# Soil Moisture Sensing Technologies

> **Type:** article
> **Domain:** Environmental Health
> **Ref:** https://www.fao.org/land-water/land/land-governance/land-resources-planning-toolbox/category/details/en/c/1036340/
> **Ingested:** 2026-05-25
> **Quality:** curated
> **Tags:** soil-moisture, agriculture, sensor, irrigation

## Abstract

Soil moisture sensing is critical for precision agriculture, irrigation management, and environmental monitoring. Measurement technologies span from gravimetric (oven-drying, the gold standard) to Time Domain Reflectometry (TDR), Frequency Domain Reflectometry (FDR/FDR), neutron probes, and remote sensing (Sentinel-1 SAR, SMAP satellite). Volumetric Water Content (VWC) is the standard measure. Low-cost capacitive sensors (e.g., SEN0193, VH400) enable dense IoT deployments but require calibration against soil type. The soil water retention curve (pF curve) relates moisture content to matric potential, essential for plant-available water calculation. Key challenges include salinity interference, sensor-to-sensor variability, and spatial heterogeneity.

## Key Concepts

- **Volumetric Water Content (VWC)** — Ratio of water volume to soil volume (m³/m³)
- **TDR/FDR** — Time/Frequency Domain Reflectometry, measures dielectric permittivity of soil
- **Matric Potential** — Energy state of water in soil (kPa or pF), determines plant availability
- **Soil Water Retention Curve** — Non-linear relationship between VWC and matric potential
- **SMAP Satellite** — NASA's Soil Moisture Active Passive, L-band radiometer (3-day global coverage)
